Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I tried to set aside about three hours a day, but with limited resources and competing responsibilities, it rarely worked out that neatly. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other things around the house. Sometimes I'd have a productive week, other times I'd barely touch my coursework until something was due.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what else was going on in my life. I'm 26 and juggling quite a bit, so regular study times felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. Deadlines definitely drove when I'd actually sit down and focus. If nothing was immediately due, I'd often let things slide and prioritize other responsibilities.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I'd missed, but honestly, without a specific reason, I didn't feel particularly motivated to browse around. The gamified tasks they introduced did make me curious enough to log in a few extra times, though they didn't always hold my attention.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, maybe when a module first started and everything felt fresh. But it never became automatic for me. Life kept getting in the way, and the platform itself didn't really pull me back in the way social media or other apps do.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful to me because I learn best by listening. I could play them while commuting or doing chores, which helped me actually absorb information despite my busy schedule. The audio format meant I could engage with the material even when I couldn't sit at a screen. Discussion forums weren't really my thing since I never posted anything.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums entirely and barely touched the reading materials posted as PDFs. Reading on a screen is hard for me, and I didn't have money to print everything out. Some of the supplementary resources felt like busywork rather than actually helpful content, so I'd skip them when I was already feeling overwhelmed.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be tested on or if it actually helped me understand something I was struggling with. The gamified tasks were interesting at first because they broke up the monotony, but once the novelty wore off, I went back to feeling bored. If something felt like it was just padding the course, I'd lose interest quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore when I was stressed or busy, which was most of the time. Required activities at least had the weight of consequences behind them, so I'd force myself to engage even when I didn't want to. With optional stuff, I'd think 'maybe later' and then later never came. The pressure of required work was sometimes the only thing that got me moving.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them offline because my internet isn't always reliable and I can't afford unlimited data. I also used YouTube videos from other educators to supplement what I was learning. Sometimes a different explanation would click better for me than what our course provided.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or replaying downloaded lectures while on the bus or waiting somewhere. None of that would show up in the LMS. I also had study sessions with a few peers where we'd discuss concepts verbally, which suits my learning style perfectly. The system only saw when I was physically logged in, not when I was actually learning.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a video lecture once and then watch it multiple times offline over the following days. My internet connection at home isn't great and I have limited data, so streaming repeatedly wasn't an option. The LMS would only register that single download, but I might have engaged with that material four or five times.
Q12: There were several points where my stress levels just peaked and I couldn't bring myself to care about the coursework. Financial pressures, family stuff, just general exhaustion from trying to manage everything. When I'm that stressed, I shut down a bit and the course material starts feeling pointless. The boredom would set in hard during those times.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with personal issues, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between studying and other urgent matters. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those modules because I didn't have the mental energy to wrestle with difficult concepts.
Q14: Many times. I'd open a video and let it play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through the course materials without actually reading or absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something productive when really I was just too drained to engage properly.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was the boredom—some tasks just felt so tedious that I'd find any excuse to do something else. My resilience isn't great, so when things got hard, avoidance became my default coping mechanism.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd have periods of almost no engagement, then a burst of frantic activity as a deadline approached. It wasn't healthy or effective, but it's how I operated. The gamified elements helped a tiny bit with spreading things out, but ultimately the fear of failing was what moved me.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did influence me, but feedback was often delayed or generic. Specific, helpful comments made me want to try harder on the next thing. But when feedback was just a grade with no explanation, it left me feeling deflated and less motivated to engage. I needed more guidance than I received.
Q18: The course structure felt a bit rigid for someone in my situation. Having 14 online courses to manage meant I was constantly juggling, and this course didn't always accommodate that reality. The pacing assumed I had more time and focus than I actually did. When modules were released in big chunks, I'd fall behind quickly.
Q19: The video content was accessible for my learning style, which helped. But the heavy reliance on written materials and discussion posts didn't work well for me. The gamified tasks were a nice attempt to make things more engaging, but they felt a bit superficial after a while. Better audio resources and more flexible deadlines would have made a huge difference.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to figure things out but struggling to actually complete work. Maybe they're confused about requirements, or they're anxious about submitting imperfect work. For me, there were times I'd log in repeatedly trying to understand what was expected, but then couldn't bring myself to actually submit anything because I wasn't confident in my work.
Q21: That somewhat reflects my experience. I did a lot of learning offline that the LMS couldn't track. Listening to downloaded lectures, studying with peers, using external resources—none of that showed up in my activity logs. My exam score was okay and I passed, even though my LMS engagement looked pretty modest. The system doesn't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when students access materials and how often they log in. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone is actively learning or just clicking through mindlessly. They also miss all the offline studying, peer discussions, and personal reflection that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and feeling like it matters. It's when I'm curious about a topic and want to understand it deeply, not just pass a test. For me, engagement is more about mental investment than physical presence on a platform. Unfortunately, I didn't feel truly engaged very often in this course—mostly just bored and going through the motions.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally and financially while trying to complete this course. It wouldn't capture the stress that made engagement feel impossible some days, or the peer conversations that actually helped me learn. It also wouldn't show that I'm an auditory learner doing my best with limited resources, trying to make it work despite everything working against me.
